executive_summary: USD/CHF trades at 0.7930 and EUR/CHF at 0.9210 as of the 2026-06-18 valuation date (latest close 2026-06-17), with the SNB's nominal effective franc near the top of its multi-year range. On the inverted convention (rising USD/CHF = CHF weaker), CHF has softened modestly against the dollar over the short window - USD/CHF +1.1% over 1M and +0.0% over 3M (CHF flat-to-weaker vs USD) - but remains a structural winner over the year, USD/CHF -2.8% over 12M (CHF stronger). Against the euro, EUR/CHF rose +0.7% 1M and +1.3% 3M (CHF weaker) yet sits -2.2% over 12M (CHF still stronger), pinned just above 0.92 - well clear of the psychological parity floor but firmly in the SNB's zone of attention. The dominant June development is macro de-escalation: a US-Iran peace framework signed around 14-15 June, with the signing ceremony hosted in Switzerland, has reopened the Strait of Hormuz, collapsed oil (Brent below $80 from a ~$97 pre-deal print) and flipped sentiment risk-on (VIX ~16.2 on 15 June from ~22 on 10 June). For a safe-haven funding currency, that de-escalation is structurally CHF-negative as crisis-driven haven flows unwind. The SNB meets today, 18 June, and is universally expected to hold its policy rate at 0.00% (Reuters poll: all 35 economists), reluctant to re-enter negative territory it calls 'undesirable'; Swiss CPI is just +0.6% YoY headline / +0.3% core (May), among the lowest in the world, and the SNB's own forecast keeps inflation inside the 0-2% band through 2028. The central tension: a near-zero-yield premier funding currency, structurally supported by a large current-account surplus, AAA credit and the world's most credible low-inflation regime, now facing a risk-on, oil-collapsing, deal-rich window that erodes the haven bid even as crowded specs are already net short ~-37k contracts. Net: tactically Neutral-to-mildly-Bearish CHF (de-escalation and zero carry dominate the 2-4 week window, but the crowded short base, EUR/CHF proximity to SNB-tolerance levels, and the lowest-vol haven status cap downside), shading to mildly Bearish strategically as consensus (UBS EUR/CHF to 0.94-0.95 by Q4) sees haven flows fading and the ECB-SNB rate gap favouring EUR. Weighted scores (tactical -0.29, strategic +0.01) place CHF mid-pack in the G10+SGD - a low-conviction, two-sided franc whose direction hinges on whether the risk-on regime holds.

valuation.trade_weighted_assessment: The SNB NEER is near the top of its multi-year band, reflecting two years of imported disinflation working through a strong franc. The SNB tolerates richness as a deliberate price-stability tool but watches for disorderly appreciation, especially in EUR/CHF.
valuation.what_is_priced_in: The market prices the franc as a persistently strong, structurally appreciating currency whose richness is a feature, not a bug, of the SNB regime. What is not fully priced is the asymmetric downside to CHF from a durable risk-on regime: if the Iran/Ukraine de-escalation holds and European growth picks up, the haven premium embedded in CHF can deflate even with the SNB at zero.
valuation.actionability: Valuation is a structural headwind to fresh CHF longs, not a tactical trade trigger - rich currencies stay rich for years under a credible disinflation regime. The signal argues against chasing CHF strength but does not, by itself, justify a short.

tactical.analysis: CHF is the lowest-yielding currency in the G10+SGD universe and the premier funding leg, and that is unambiguously CHF-negative on the carry channel. The SNB policy rate is 0.00%, expected to be confirmed today (18 June) with all 35 economists in the Reuters poll forecasting a hold. Against this, the US Fed funds effective is ~3.63%, the ECB depo 2.25%, the BoE 3.75%, the RBA 4.35% and Norges Bank 4.25% - CHF pays 225-435bps to be held against every major peer. The Swiss 2Y yield is approximately -0.05% and the 10Y ~0.35%, the deepest-discount curve in G10, leaving CH-US spreads near -412bps at both tenors and CH-DE 10Y at -235bps. In a risk-on regime where funding currencies are sold to finance carry, the structurally negative differential dominates the tactical window and argues for CHF weakness (USD/CHF and EUR/CHF up). The SNB's reluctance to cut into negative territory caps how much further the rate disadvantage can deepen, but it also removes any near-term prospect of CHF support from a relative-rate shift. The 2s10s curve at ~+40bps signals no stress, just a compressed low-rate world. Net, the rate factor is a clear tactical headwind for the franc.

tactical.what_is_priced: OIS fully prices a 0% hold today and through end-2026 - there is no rate surprise embedded. The edge is in the asymmetry: markets underprice the small but rising probability (ING) that the SNB is eventually forced back to negative rates if disinflation deepens, which would be a fresh CHF-negative leg not in the curve.

strategic.analysis: Over 1-6 months the rate disadvantage is structural and durable: the SNB will sit at 0% while the Fed holds in the high-3s and the ECB on an extended pause at 2.25%. The SNB-ECB gap of -225bps is the mechanical driver of EUR/CHF, and with the ECB explicitly on hold (which UBS called 'music to the SNB's ears'), the differential favours EUR over CHF and supports a higher EUR/CHF over the horizon. The franc's persistent negative carry means CHF-funded carry trades remain attractive as long as volatility stays low, draining CHF on the flow channel. The counterweight is the SNB's near-zero floor: it has run out of conventional easing room without going negative, so the rate gap cannot widen much further and any global risk shock would see the carry trades unwind violently in CHF's favour. The forward curve embeds the negative carry, so CHF appreciates in the forwards (forward points favour CHF) - a partial offset to the spot carry drag. Swiss yields will stay anchored near zero given sub-1% inflation forecasts through 2028. The strategic rate verdict is mildly CHF-bearish, with conviction tempered by the floored policy rate and the embedded forward appreciation.

carry_analysis.carry_regime: Carry is 'working' in the June risk-on regime - low vol plus de-escalation rewards funding in CHF and going long high-yielders, which drags CHF weaker. A regime flip to risk-off would instantly invert this, making the CHF short the painful side.
carry_analysis.breakeven_move: On EUR/CHF, the +225bps annual carry of being short CHF/long EUR equals ~+0.0021 of spot over 30 days (~21 pips); EUR/CHF must fall more than ~21 pips from 0.9210 (to ~0.9189) in a month before the carry on a short-CHF position is erased - a thin cushion that a single risk-off day can wipe out.

tactical.analysis: Swiss inflation is the lowest in the developed world and that, paradoxically, is a near-term CHF-negative because it removes any case for SNB tightening and keeps alive the risk of a forced return to negative rates. Headline CPI rose just +0.6% YoY in May 2026 (below the 0.8% expected), with core (ex fresh/seasonal food and energy) at only +0.3% YoY - the softest core since July 2021. The strong franc is the engine of this disinflation: imported goods (23% of the basket) have been falling ~1.3% YoY, while domestic prices rise only ~0.4%. Growth is sub-trend but positive: Q1 GDP +0.66% QoQ / +0.59% YoY, and the KOF Economic Barometer at 98.0 (May) signals subdued momentum. SECO forecasts below-average 2026 GDP of 1.0%. The data flow gives the SNB no reason to support the franc via rates and every reason to tolerate or quietly welcome modest CHF weakness to lift import prices. Relative to peers running 2-4% inflation (US 4.25%, AUD 4.05%, GBP 3.3%), the Swiss inflation undershoot is the deepest disinflation differential in G10. The near-term inflation/growth signal is therefore mildly CHF-bearish.

tactical.what_is_priced: The market prices Swiss inflation as comfortably low and the SNB as on hold - no surprise embedded. The edge: a downside CPI surprise toward zero or negative prints (ING flags Q1-Q3 forecasts of 0.1-0.3%) would revive negative-rate speculation, a fresh CHF-bearish catalyst the curve barely reflects.

strategic.analysis: Strategically the inflation/growth story is more two-sided for the franc. The SNB's own conditional forecast keeps inflation inside the 0-2% band through 2028 (0.5% 2026, 0.5% 2027, 0.6% 2028), implying a stable, non-threatening regime that neither forces easing nor invites tightening. Growth at ~1.0% (SECO 2026) is sub-trend but resilient given Switzerland's defensive sector mix (pharma, financials, luxury, precision engineering) which is less cyclically exposed than the eurozone. Crucially, the franc's strength is the disinflation mechanism: if CHF weakens, imported disinflation reverses and inflation drifts back toward the middle of the band - a self-correcting feedback that limits how persistently CHF can fall on this channel. Over 6 months the collapse in oil (post-Iran deal) is a further disinflationary impulse for an energy importer, reinforcing low headline CPI. The net effect is balanced: low inflation argues against CHF support, but the self-stabilising FX-inflation loop and defensive growth profile argue against sustained CHF weakness. We assign Neutral, with the stalemate broken only by a decisive move outside the 0-2% band.

tactical.analysis: Risk appetite is the most acute tactical driver for the franc right now, and it points CHF-bearish. The June regime is decisively risk-on: the US-Iran peace framework (signed ~14-15 June) reopened the Strait of Hormuz, collapsed Brent below $80 from a ~$97 pre-deal print, and drove the VIX to ~16.2 on 15 June from ~22 on 10 June. For a safe-haven currency, that de-escalation unwinds the crisis premium that had accumulated in CHF during the spring oil-supply shock. CHF's risk beta is negative - it strengthens in risk-off and weakens in risk-on - so a sharp VIX decline mechanically erodes the haven bid. Equity markets are firm and credit spreads contained, the classic backdrop in which funding currencies like CHF are sold. The franc's safe-haven channels (neutrality, current-account surplus, AAA credit) are intact but dormant when fear recedes. The clean read is that the current risk environment is hurting CHF and will continue to as long as the de-escalation narrative holds. The main caveat is the fragility of the Iran deal (Iranian state media flagged Hormuz tolls free for only 60 days), which keeps a re-escalation tail alive.

tactical.what_is_priced: Markets have rapidly priced the de-escalation as durable, compressing the haven premium in CHF. The edge is two-sided: if the deal holds, CHF has further to fall as residual haven hedges unwind; if it wobbles, the crowded short and cheap CHF vol make a haven-squeeze rally violent and under-hedged.

strategic.analysis: Over 1-6 months the risk-sentiment signal is genuinely two-sided. The base case is that the Iran/Ukraine de-escalation broadly holds, equities grind higher and CHF's haven premium stays compressed - mildly CHF-bearish. But the strategic horizon spans multiple potential shock windows (US fiscal/political events, the durability of the Hormuz arrangement past 60 days, European banking or sovereign stress), any of which could flip the regime risk-off and reignite the CHF bid. CHF is a structural safe haven precisely because these tail events recur; over six months the probability that at least one materialises is non-trivial. The franc holds gains longer than the yen once a haven move starts (per the currency brief), so even an intermittent risk-off episode can leave CHF stronger than a pure risk-on path would imply. Balancing a benign central path against recurring tail risk, the strategic risk factor is Neutral - it neither reliably supports nor undermines CHF over the horizon, and conviction is low.

tactical.analysis: Positioning is the one clear tactical counterweight to the bearish-CHF bundle, and it leans CHF-bullish on a contrarian basis. CFTC data for the week to 9-12 June show leveraged funds net short ~-10.8k CHF contracts (having added shorts, -1.2k on the week) and asset managers net short ~-36.6k, for an aggregate non-commercial net short around -37k - a clean, one-way short book in the premier funding currency. Specs deepened the short into the risk-on move (the InvestingCom proxy showed net specs sliding to ~-36.7k from -32.9k). Total open interest of ~115k is moderate for CHF. The combined short sits around the 20th percentile of its multi-year range - crowded enough that fresh CHF weakness has fewer marginal sellers to drive it, and any risk-off catalyst would force a sharp short-covering rally (CHF strength). CHF squeezes are historically violent given the haven beta. The signal is therefore contrarian-bullish: positioning warns that the easy CHF-short money has been made and the risk/reward of pressing the short is deteriorating. Conviction is moderate because -37k is crowded but not a historic wash-out extreme.

tactical.what_is_priced: The crowded short prices a continuation of CHF weakness, which is the consensus carry trade. The contrarian edge: with positioning already short and only modest fresh-seller capacity, the asymmetry tilts toward a squeeze on any risk-off catalyst - the market is underpricing how quickly the short unwinds if the Iran deal wobbles.

strategic.analysis: Over 1-6 months positioning is a weaker signal because the structural CHF short is a semi-permanent feature of a 0%-yielding funding currency, not a tactical extreme that must mean-revert. Asset managers and leveraged funds being net short CHF is the natural resting state when the SNB sits at zero and carry trades are funded in francs; it can persist for quarters without unwinding. The ~20th-percentile reading is crowded but well short of the sub-5th-percentile extremes that have historically preceded forced unwinds. SNB sight deposits and the ~CHF 715-900bn reserve stock are the other side of the flow ledger: the SNB stands ready to lean against franc appreciation but has signalled it prefers the rate tool to active intervention in 2026, so it is not currently adding CHF supply aggressively. Without a clear extreme or an active intervention flow, positioning gives no strong directional steer over the strategic horizon. The contrarian squeeze risk remains a tail, not a base case. We assign Neutral.

strategic.what_is_priced: The persistent short is priced as a stable carry-funding posture. Over six months we see it as neither a reliable contrarian buy nor a momentum sell - it is a coiled spring that only releases on a genuine risk-off catalyst, so the strategic positioning signal is Neutral.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical is Bullish (55) on a contrarian read - the crowded short and violent-squeeze potential matter most in the 2-4 week window where a catalyst can fire; strategic is Neutral (50) because a structural funding-currency short is the natural resting state and lacks mean-reversion pull over 1-6 months.
factors.contrarian_analysis_tactical: Positioning is moderately stretched short at ~20th percentile but not at a historic wash-out (which for CHF begins below the ~5th-10th percentile given its smaller open interest than EUR/JPY). The unwind trigger would be any risk-off catalyst - an Iran-deal collapse, a sharp equity selloff, or European banking/sovereign stress - that reignites the haven bid and forces specs to cover. A squeeze would be violent: CHF's negative risk beta plus cheap vol (IV ~30th pct) and the 2015 tail precedent mean EUR/CHF and USD/CHF could gap several big-figures lower (CHF stronger) in days. The other side of the trade sees a structurally cheap-to-fund currency that simply has no yield support and will keep drifting weaker in a calm regime - the carry crowd's core thesis.
factors.contrarian_analysis_strategic: Strategically the short is not stretched enough to be a standalone contrarian buy - a 0% funding currency is supposed to be net short, and ~20th percentile can persist for quarters. The unwind catalyst over 1-6 months is the recurrence of at least one risk-off shock window (Hormuz toll expiry, US fiscal/political event, EU stress). If none materialises, the short simply rides the carry; if one does, the squeeze is sharp but likely retraces once calm returns. The contrarian case is therefore a tail hedge, not a strategic directional thesis - which is why we keep strategic positioning Neutral.

tactical.analysis: Switzerland's external position is a structural CHF tailwind that barely moves on a 2-4 week horizon, so it is tactically Neutral. The current account is in persistent surplus - the OECD cache shows +3.0% of GDP (Q4-2025), and on broader Swiss definitions that adjust for gold-trade distortions the underlying surplus is materially larger (historically 5-8% of GDP). That surplus generates a structural bid for CHF that does not switch on and off week to week. The June oil collapse (Brent below $80) is a mild terms-of-trade positive for energy-importer Switzerland, improving the trade balance at the margin, but the effect is slow-moving and dwarfed by the risk-sentiment channel in the tactical window. Switzerland's exports - pharma, watches, precision engineering, plus financial and commodity-trading services - are price-inelastic and high-value, insulating the external account from short-term swings. Gold trade flows (Switzerland is the world's largest refining hub) inject monthly noise but net out over time. None of this generates a tactical directional signal, so the factor is Neutral near term with the structural surplus a standing background support.

tactical.what_is_priced: The market treats the Swiss surplus as a permanent given and does not trade it tactically. There is no near-term mispricing to exploit; the surplus is a slow structural support that matters far more at the strategic horizon than in the 2-4 week window.

strategic.analysis: At the strategic horizon, where its weight jumps to 0.16, the external balance is the franc's single strongest structural support and the foundation of its decades-long real appreciation. Switzerland runs one of the largest and most durable current-account surpluses in the world, driven by a goods-trade surplus (pharma, watches, precision engineering), a services surplus (financial services and the Geneva/Zug commodity-trading complex), and a very large primary-income surplus from a huge stock of overseas investments. This persistent surplus creates structural, price-insensitive demand for CHF that mechanically pushes the real exchange rate higher over time - the fundamental reason the franc is chronically rich and keeps appreciating despite zero yield. The oil collapse improves the terms of trade for an energy importer, modestly widening the surplus. This factor directly opposes the bearish rate and risk signals: over 1-6 months the balance-of-payments bid is a powerful CHF-bullish counterweight that limits how far and how durably the franc can weaken. It is the core reason a strategic CHF-bearish view must stay low-conviction. The surplus does not generate fast appreciation, but it provides a firm floor under the franc.

strategic.what_is_priced: The structural surplus is well known but its persistence is repeatedly underpriced by tactical traders who chase CHF weakness on the carry trade and are surprised when the franc refuses to fall durably. The edge is recognising that the balance-of-payments bid caps strategic CHF downside and keeps the franc structurally rich - a key reason to fade aggressive CHF-bearish targets.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical is Neutral (55) because the slow-moving surplus generates no 2-4 week signal; strategic is Bullish (68) because at a 0.16 weight the persistent balance-of-payments bid becomes the franc's dominant structural support and the main counterweight to the bearish rate/risk factors.

tactical.analysis: The SNB's guidance is deliberately minimal - only four meetings a year and a three-member Governing Board - so today's 18 June assessment is the dominant near-term communication event, and it is expected to deliver a 0% hold with no rate surprise. The tactical signal hinges entirely on the tone and the updated conditional inflation forecast accompanying the decision. The SNB has been consistent and credible: it reaffirmed in March that inflation sits 'within the price stability range for the entire forecast horizon' and has repeatedly stressed it wants to avoid negative rates, calling their effects 'undesirable' and the bar for them 'high.' That messaging is mildly CHF-neutral-to-bearish - it caps both the prospect of hikes and signals tolerance of a softer franc to support import prices. The key watch is whether the SNB sounds more open to FX intervention or to eventual negative rates if the franc strengthens; in 2026 it has leaned toward the rate tool and away from active intervention. With the outcome near-fully telegraphed, the tactical guidance factor is Neutral - the risk is a tone surprise in either direction rather than a directional steer.

tactical.what_is_priced: Markets fully price a 0% hold and benign guidance. The tactical edge is in the forecast revision and tone: a downward inflation-forecast revision toward zero (reviving negative-rate odds) would be CHF-bearish, while any hint of renewed intervention readiness against franc strength would also cap CHF - both tail tone risks, not the base case.

strategic.analysis: Strategically the SNB is highly credible but its guidance gives little directional steer because it is reactive and data-dependent rather than pre-committed. The central message - rates at 0%, inflation comfortably in-band through 2028, reluctance to go negative - implies a long policy pause that neither supports nor pressures the franc on the rate-expectations channel. The SNB's dual focus on price stability 'with due account of economic developments' means it implicitly tolerates a strong franc as a disinflation tool but will lean against disorderly appreciation, a two-sided reaction function that anchors EUR/CHF in a managed range rather than driving a trend. Credibility is high but the 2015 floor-removal remains a structural reminder that the SNB can surprise abruptly, embedding a permanent tail-risk premium in CHF options. Over 1-6 months the guidance factor is Neutral: the SNB is a stabilising, range-keeping force, not a source of directional CHF momentum. The main strategic swing factor is whether persistent sub-band inflation eventually forces the SNB to choose between negative rates (CHF-bearish) and renewed large-scale intervention (CHF-bearish), a decision that would only crystallise later in the horizon.

strategic.what_is_priced: The market prices the SNB as a credible, stabilising, range-keeping central bank - correctly. The strategic edge is recognising the embedded surprise risk: with the rate floored near zero, the SNB's eventual choice between negative rates and intervention is a latent CHF-bearish catalyst the market discounts because the SNB keeps deferring it.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Both horizons are Neutral on guidance because the SNB telegraphs a credible 0% pause that gives no directional steer; tactical confidence (60) edges strategic (58) only because today's decision and forecast revision are a concrete near-term event, whereas the strategic latent negative-rate/intervention choice is more diffuse.

tactical.analysis: The dominant June event - the US-Iran peace framework - is CHF-bearish because it removes the geopolitical fear that had been bidding the franc as a haven. The deal was signed around 14-15 June with the signing ceremony hosted in Switzerland itself, reopening the Strait of Hormuz, crashing oil and lifting risk sentiment. For a safe-haven currency, the resolution of a major conflict is a direct negative as crisis-driven capital refuges unwind. The franc's haven status, normally an asset, is dormant in a de-escalating world. The key tactical caveat is the fragility of the arrangement: Iranian state media indicated Hormuz would be toll-free for only 60 days, and discrepancies between the US and Iranian understandings have surfaced, leaving a live re-escalation tail that could snap the haven bid back on. Swiss domestic political risk is essentially nil. So the net tactical event signal is CHF-bearish driven by the de-escalation, with an asymmetric upside tail if the deal unravels. Event-vol pricing is cheap (CHF IV ~30th pct), making the re-escalation hedge attractively priced.

tactical.what_is_priced: The market has priced the Iran deal as a durable de-escalation, compressing CHF's geopolitical premium. The edge: the 60-day toll caveat and reported US-Iran discrepancies mean the deal is more fragile than priced, so cheap long-CHF-vol hedges offer asymmetric payoff if it wobbles.

strategic.analysis: Over 1-6 months event risk is genuinely two-sided for the franc. The benign path sees the Iran framework hold and the Ukraine-Russia track progress, sustaining the risk-on regime that pressures CHF. But the strategic horizon contains multiple binary windows: the 60-day Hormuz toll arrangement expires within it, US political and fiscal flashpoints recur, and European stress (banking, sovereign, or Swiss-EU relations) can flare. Switzerland's adoption of EU sanctions against Russia has tested its traditional neutrality, a slow-burn factor that could marginally erode the structural haven premium if it deepens. Conversely, any major geopolitical shock would reignite the CHF bid sharply. UBS explicitly flags de-escalation (Iran, Ukraine) as the key downside risk to the franc, while the recurrence of shocks is the upside. Because these forces roughly offset and the timing of any flare-up is unknowable, the strategic event factor is Neutral with low conviction - it functions as embedded optionality rather than a directional thesis.

strategic.what_is_priced: Consensus prices durable de-escalation as the central path, which compresses the franc's geopolitical premium. We see roughly symmetric two-sided risk over six months, so the strategic stance is Neutral - the haven optionality is undervalued but its timing is too uncertain to assign a directional bias.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical is Bearish (60) because the in-progress Iran de-escalation is actively unwinding the haven bid now; strategic is Neutral (52) because over 1-6 months the recurrence of shock windows (Hormuz toll expiry, US/EU flashpoints) gives the haven bid offsetting optionality.

tactical.analysis: Swiss fiscal and sovereign fundamentals are the gold standard and a standing structural support for the franc, though they generate no fresh tactical catalyst. Switzerland holds an AAA rating from all agencies with a stable outlook (S&P, Scope affirmed AAA in 2026), and the debt brake constitutionally constrains structural deficits. General-government metrics are pristine: federal net debt was CHF 140bn at end-2025, ~16% of GDP - among the lowest in the developed world. The government runs near-balanced budgets structurally. This fortress balance sheet is precisely why CHF is a safe-haven refuge: in a crisis, capital flees to the issuer least likely to default or inflate away its debt, and Switzerland is that issuer. Tactically nothing is changing - no auctions of concern, no rating reviews, no fiscal shocks - so the factor is a quiet, persistent CHF-positive rather than a near-term driver. The one watch item is the UBS capital-rules saga (an extra ~$20bn requirement at the Swiss unit, ordinances due Jan-2027), a banking-sector rather than sovereign issue. Net tactical: mildly CHF-bullish but dormant.

tactical.what_is_priced: The market fully prices Switzerland's AAA fortress status - it is the baseline assumption behind CHF's haven role. There is no tactical mispricing; the factor's value is as a permanent structural backstop that prevents fiscal risk from ever being a CHF-negative.

strategic.analysis: Strategically, Switzerland's fiscal and sovereign profile is an unambiguous and durable CHF tailwind, and its weight rises to 0.07. The AAA rating, ~16% debt/GDP and the debt-brake regime mean Switzerland has essentially zero sovereign credit risk and ample fiscal space - the polar opposite of the US, UK, France or Japan, where rising debt and term-premium concerns periodically pressure currencies. This fiscal supremacy is a core pillar of the franc's structural appreciation and haven status: it guarantees that in any future debt or sovereign-stress episode elsewhere, capital will rotate toward CHF. Over 1-6 months the only live items are the UBS capital framework (a financial-stability positive that strengthens the banking system even as it irritates UBS, with ordinances due Jan-2027) and the ongoing Swiss-EU bilateral negotiations, neither of which threatens the sovereign. The factor reinforces the strategic external-balance support and is one of the strongest reasons to keep any bearish-CHF view modest. Fiscal risk simply is not a CHF-negative on any realistic horizon.

strategic.what_is_priced: Switzerland's fiscal fortress is fully and correctly priced as the bedrock of CHF's haven appeal. The strategic edge is comparative: as other DM sovereigns face deteriorating debt dynamics, Switzerland's relative fiscal supremacy will keep attracting structural CHF demand, a slow tailwind the carry-focused market routinely underweights.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Both horizons are Bullish CHF on fiscal strength; strategic confidence (75) exceeds tactical (72) because the fiscal fortress matters more at a 0.07 strategic weight as a durable comparative advantage versus deteriorating DM peers, whereas tactically it is a dormant background support with no near-term catalyst.

tactical.analysis: The structural and volatility picture is two-sided and best read as Neutral. USD/CHF is range-bound between roughly 0.785 support and 0.800 resistance, with spot at 0.7930 sitting just above flattening 50d/100d MAs but below the 200d (~0.800) - a tangled, transitioning MA configuration that signals consolidation, not a clean trend. EUR/CHF is grinding higher off the 0.905 area toward 0.92-0.925, far below the 1.0000 parity floor, so there is no SNB-tolerance stress in the structure. Volatility is the more informative dimension: CHF 1M implied vol is ~6.8% and 3M ~7.1%, near the 30th percentile of the past year - options are relatively cheap. Realised 20d vol (~6.2%) sits just below implied, leaving a small event premium around today's SNB meeting; the term structure is in mild contango (no large near-term event premium beyond the SNB). The 25-delta risk reversal is roughly flat-to-slightly CHF-call bid, reflecting residual haven hedging demand even amid risk-on. The single most important caveat is CHF's fat-tail problem: average vol is low but tail moves (2015: EUR/CHF -30% intraday) are extreme, so realised calm understates true risk. Structure gives no directional signal; it argues for owning cheap CHF optionality rather than pressing spot.

tactical.what_is_priced: With IV near the 30th percentile, the options market prices continued calm and is not pricing a near-term break. The edge: cheap vol plus CHF's fat-tail history make long CHF gamma an under-priced hedge against a haven squeeze, even though spot structure itself gives no directional bias.

strategic.analysis: Over 1-6 months the structural and vol picture remains Neutral. The medium-term trend is a low-vol grind: USD/CHF has appreciated CHF over the year (spot below the 200d) but is consolidating, while EUR/CHF is range-managed by the SNB's reaction function well below parity. There is no decisive trend break in either direction, and the MA configuration is tangled rather than stacked, implying choppy rather than trending price action. Strategic vol is structurally low for CHF - it is a low-vol funding/haven currency - but the fat-tail risk is permanent and arguably underpriced at the 6-month horizon, where the probability of at least one vol-spiking shock (the Hormuz toll expiry, a risk-off episode, an SNB surprise) is meaningful. The contango term structure and ~30th-percentile IV suggest the market is not bracing for such an event. For a strategic CHF view, the structure neither confirms nor denies the modestly bearish fundamental lean; it simply frames the franc as a range-bound, low-vol asset with embedded tail convexity. We keep it Neutral.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Both horizons are Neutral - the structure gives no directional signal at either - with tactical confidence (55) marginally above strategic (50) because the near-term SNB event premium and tighter range levels are more concrete than the diffuse medium-term grind.
factors.expression_assessment: With CHF implied vol near the 30th percentile and a chronic fat-tail history (the 2015 floor removal moved EUR/CHF ~30% intraday), options are cheap relative to the franc's true risk distribution - the standout structural conclusion is that any view, but especially the haven-squeeze tail, is better owned via long CHF optionality (gamma) than spot. Because RV (~6.2%) sits only modestly below IV (~6.8%), the market is not badly mispricing near-term realised moves, so a spot expression of the modestly bearish carry view is also efficient for the directional component. The cleanest synthesis: express the low-conviction bearish-CHF carry lean in spot (e.g. long EUR/CHF) while funding a cheap long-CHF gamma hedge against the de-escalation reversing - the contango term structure and low IV make that convexity inexpensive. Avoid selling CHF vol despite the carry temptation: the asymmetric tail makes short-vol the wrong side for a currency that gaps violently.

tactical.analysis: USD funding conditions are benign in the June risk-on regime, which is broadly neutral for CHF tactically. SOFR prints at ~3.69% and trades stably near IORB with no dislocation; the Fed balance sheet stands at ~$6.73tn (week of 10 June) with QT having slowed, and there is no evidence of USD swap-line usage that would signal stress. Cross-currency basis is well-behaved: 3M EUR/USD basis is mildly negative (a modest structural USD premium) and the CHF/USD basis is similarly contained, meaning Swiss institutions - significant USD borrowers - face no funding squeeze. In calm USD-liquidity conditions, the dollar-smile framework puts the USD at the comfortable middle/right (US growth solid, no crisis), which is mildly CHF-negative because CHF, like JPY, strengthens at the crisis left-tail and weakens in goldilocks. The flat funding backdrop removes the one channel through which a USD shortage would force CHF-funded carry trades to unwind (CHF-positive). With no funding stress and the dollar firm-but-orderly, the tactical liquidity factor gives no directional CHF signal - Neutral. The key watch is any sudden basis widening that would flag the early stage of a funding squeeze.

tactical.what_is_priced: The market prices ample, orderly USD liquidity with no funding stress. The edge is asymmetric: a sudden basis widening or swap-line drawdown would flip this factor sharply CHF-positive (carry unwind + haven bid), a tail the calm pricing ignores.

strategic.analysis: Over 1-6 months USD liquidity is a swing factor that currently reads Neutral but carries asymmetric CHF-positive tail risk. The base case is continued orderly funding: the Fed holds rates in the high-3s with QT having largely run its course, reserves remain adequate, and global USD demand is seasonally manageable outside year-end. In that world the dollar stays firm-to-strong (right side of the smile on US growth exceptionalism), which is a mild headwind for CHF versus USD specifically. But the strategic horizon spans potential stress windows - quarter/year-end funding pressure, an equity or credit shock, or EM USD demand spikes - any of which would tighten dollar liquidity, widen cross-currency basis and force the unwind of CHF-funded carry trades, mechanically strengthening the franc. CHF behaves like JPY here: it is a left-tail winner when dollar liquidity seizes. Because the benign base case (mild CHF-negative) and the funding-stress tail (sharp CHF-positive) roughly offset over six months, the factor is Neutral with the bias being convex - the downside for CHF is capped while the upside tail is large. This convexity is another reason to keep the strategic bearish-CHF view modest.

strategic.what_is_priced: Consensus prices abundant, orderly USD liquidity through the horizon. We agree on the base case but note the market under-prices the recurrent funding-stress tail that would force a CHF-positive carry unwind - the reason the strategic stance is Neutral-with-convex-upside rather than cleanly bearish.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Both horizons are Neutral because USD funding is currently benign and gives no directional CHF signal; the small confidence step (tactical 55 vs strategic 52) reflects that near-term conditions are observably calm while the 6-month path carries more uncertain funding-stress windows.

weighted_score.interpretation: A net tactical score of -0.286 is a moderate bearish-CHF reading, driven overwhelmingly by the 0.24-weighted negative-carry rate factor (-0.168) reinforced by ultra-low inflation (-0.104) and the risk-on de-escalation (-0.068). The only material offsets are the contrarian positioning long (+0.055) and the fiscal fortress (+0.029), while external balance, guidance, structure and USD funding net to zero.
weighted_score.mapping_to_conviction: A -0.286 score maps to a modest bearish lean. Conviction is set at 2 (not higher) because the bearish carry/risk thesis collides with a crowded short, a structural BoP bid, cheap haven optionality and the franc's violent-squeeze history - the path is real but the asymmetry and offsets keep conviction low.

tactical.rationale: At tactical weights the franc's fate is dominated by the 0.24-weighted policy-and-rates factor, and there CHF is the unambiguous loser: at 0.00% it is the lowest-yielding G10+SGD currency and the premier funding leg, paying 225-435bps to be held against every major peer. That negative carry is amplified by the second-heaviest factor, inflation and growth (0.18), where +0.6% headline / +0.3% core CPI gives the SNB no reason to support the franc and keeps alive the risk of a forced return to negative rates. The third leg is risk appetite (0.10): the US-Iran peace deal, signed in Switzerland around 14-15 June, collapsed oil below $80 and drove the VIX to ~16.2, a textbook risk-on impulse that unwinds CHF's haven premium. These three reinforcing bearish factors (-0.168, -0.104, -0.068) define the path of least resistance: a gentle CHF-weaker grind, USD/CHF and EUR/CHF drifting higher. They are only partly offset by the contrarian positioning signal (+0.055) - specs are crowded net short ~-37k - and the fiscal fortress (+0.029). The critical factor interaction is the tension between the risk-on carry trade (CHF-bearish) and the crowded short plus cheap vol (which make any risk-off reversal a violent CHF-positive squeeze), so the bearish view is directionally right but fragile. What is priced: OIS fully embeds a 0% SNB hold today and the market prices durable de-escalation, so there is no rate or event surprise to harvest on the downside; the edge is the under-priced re-escalation tail and the under-appreciated structural BoP bid that caps how far CHF can fall. Key near-term catalysts are concentrated: the SNB decision today (18 June), the next Swiss CPI in early July, and the 60-day Hormuz toll window. Conviction drivers are thin - this is a low-conviction lean because every bearish argument has a credible offset - which is why net stance is Bearish but conviction only 2. Actionability is modest: the cleanest expression is long EUR/CHF for the carry-plus-ECB-hold tailwind, sized small and paired with a cheap long-CHF gamma hedge, rather than an aggressive directional short.

g10_ranking.commentary: Tactically CHF ranks 8th: in a risk-on, carry-friendly window the zero-yield funding currency lags the high-yielders (AUD, NOK) and the firm dollar, beating only the fellow ultra-low-yield funder JPY, the oil-losing CAD and high-unemployment SEK. The ranking would jump sharply higher on any risk-off flip, reflecting CHF's haven optionality.

Expect EUR/CHF to edge from 0.921 toward 0.925-0.930 and USD/CHF to hold the 0.790-0.800 range, with the SNB hold today removing the last near-term event hurdle. Volatility should stay compressed (IV ~30th percentile) absent a shock, so pullbacks in EUR/CHF will be shallow and bought by carry funders. The dominant risk to this path is a sudden risk-off catalyst that fires the crowded short into a sharp, multi-big-figure CHF rally - a spike-and-fade shape rather than a sustained reversal. Net, a gentle drift CHF-weaker punctuated by sharp but likely temporary haven squeezes.

risk_reward.base_case_payoff: Long EUR/CHF base case +90 to +110 pips (0.921 -> 0.930-0.932) over 2-4 weeks
risk_reward.downside_case_loss: Haven-squeeze risk -150 to -170 pips (0.921 -> 0.905) if the Iran deal wobbles
risk_reward.risk_reward_ratio: ~0.6:1 (base +100 pips vs squeeze -160 pips) - unfavourable on a naked short-CHF/long-EUR-CHF position
risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: The asymmetry is adverse: the bearish-CHF carry trade earns a thin, slow premium but risks a violent squeeze, which is exactly why conviction is low and the recommendation is to pair any long EUR/CHF with a cheap long-CHF gamma hedge rather than run it naked.

weighted_score.interpretation: A net strategic score of +0.013 is essentially flat - a hair on the bullish side of neutral. The heavy negative-carry rate factor (-0.149) is almost exactly offset by the now 0.16-weighted external-balance surplus (+0.109) and the fiscal fortress (+0.053), with inflation, guidance, risk, positioning, structure and USD funding all neutral.
weighted_score.mapping_to_conviction: A +0.013 score sits on the neutral-to-marginally-bullish boundary. We override the raw arithmetic to a Bearish net stance at conviction 2 because the consensus (UBS EUR/CHF to 0.94-0.95) and the dominant rate disadvantage argue for modest CHF weakness, while the near-zero score itself justifies the very low conviction - this is close to a coin flip with a slight CHF-weaker tilt.

strategic.rationale: At strategic weights the franc's analysis becomes a genuine tug-of-war that nets close to flat, which is the central finding. The 0.24-weighted rate factor remains the heaviest single force and stays CHF-bearish (-0.149): a 0%-floored SNB versus an ECB on hold at 2.25% and a Fed in the high-3s means CHF keeps paying away carry, and the SNB-ECB gap mechanically supports a higher EUR/CHF. But the strategic re-weighting transforms the picture by lifting external balance to 0.16, and there the persistent current-account surplus (+3% of GDP headline, materially larger on underlying Swiss measures) delivers a powerful +0.109 CHF-bullish counterweight, joined by the fiscal fortress (+0.053). These structural supports nearly neutralise the carry drag, leaving a net score of +0.013 - effectively flat. The decisive factor interaction is precisely this clash: the carry/expectations channel says sell the franc, while the balance-of-payments and sovereign-credit channels say the franc is structurally supported and chronically rich. We resolve it to a modestly Bearish stance at conviction 2, aligning with consensus (UBS sees EUR/CHF rising to 0.94-0.95 by Q4 on ECB hold and fading haven flows) but conceding the near-coin-flip nature of the call. The path of least resistance is a slow EUR/CHF grind higher, capped by the structural CHF bid that repeatedly frustrates carry traders chasing CHF weakness. What is priced: the market prices a stable 0% SNB, a benign inflation regime and continued de-escalation; the edge is recognising that the structural surplus and fiscal supremacy cap strategic CHF downside, so aggressive bearish targets should be faded. Key catalysts span the September and December SNB meetings, the Hormuz toll expiry, and any DM fiscal-stress episode that would reignite the CHF bid. Conviction is low (2) because the factors so nearly offset; actionability is modest - a small long EUR/CHF position toward consensus targets, explicitly hedged for the recurrent haven-squeeze tail.

g10_ranking.commentary: Strategically CHF ranks mid-pack at 6th: the structural surplus, AAA credit and chronic appreciation tendency keep it well ahead of the soft-growth/high-debt cohort (JPY, CAD, GBP) and a potentially softening USD, but the zero yield leaves it behind the high-carry NOK/AUD and the appreciating SGD. It is a defensive, range-bound hold rather than a strategic outperformer in a risk-on world.

base_case.outcome: EUR/CHF drifts toward 0.94-0.95 (CHF weaker) over 1-6 months per consensus, USD/CHF holds 0.78-0.81

Risk-on regime broadly persists with intermittent, retraced risk-off episodes
Structural surplus caps the pace of CHF weakness - a grind, not a slide
base_case.path: A slow, surplus-capped EUR/CHF grind higher toward 0.94-0.95, punctuated by sharp but temporary haven squeezes on each risk-off flare. Shape: shallow uptrend with downside spikes.

strategic.path_expectation: The strategic path is a slow, structurally-capped EUR/CHF grind higher toward the 0.94-0.95 consensus zone, with USD/CHF range-bound around 0.78-0.81 as the firm-USD and zero-carry forces roughly offset the structural CHF bid. Expect the franc's persistent surplus and fiscal supremacy to repeatedly frustrate the pace of weakness, so any move toward 0.95 EUR/CHF will be grinding and frequently interrupted. The most likely shape is a shallow CHF-weaker drift overlaid with sharp, retraced haven-squeeze spikes whenever a risk-off window opens (Hormuz toll expiry, DM stress). Vol should stay structurally low between shocks. The probability that at least one risk-off episode interrupts the drift over six months is high, so the realised path will be choppy rather than a clean trend - reinforcing the low conviction.

risk_reward.base_case_payoff: Long EUR/CHF base case +200 to +290 pips (0.921 -> 0.94-0.95) over 1-6 months toward consensus
risk_reward.downside_case_loss: Haven-bid upside-case loss -200 pips (0.921 -> 0.901) on a major risk-off shock
risk_reward.risk_reward_ratio: ~1.2:1 (base +250 pips vs shock -200 pips) - only marginally favourable, consistent with low conviction
risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: The distribution is close to symmetric with a fat left tail (sharp CHF strength on any shock), so the modest bearish-CHF carry view offers only slightly positive expected value and must be hedged against the recurrent haven-squeeze tail.

consensus_divergence.consensus_view: Consensus (led by UBS) sees EUR/CHF rising to 0.94 then 0.95 by 2H-2026 and USD/CHF stabilising around 0.78, on the view that the ECB's extended hold favours EUR over CHF and that the franc's 2025 safe-haven flows fade as macro clarity (Iran, Ukraine de-escalation) emerges. The Reuters poll consensus is an SNB hold at 0% through end-2026.
consensus_divergence.our_view: We agree directionally - modestly Bearish CHF on both horizons - but at materially lower conviction (2/5) than the confident UBS EUR/CHF-to-0.95 call. We emphasise that the structural current-account surplus and fiscal supremacy cap the pace of CHF weakness, so we expect a grinding, frequently-interrupted move rather than a clean glide to 0.95.
consensus_divergence.divergence_magnitude: Direction-aligned but conviction- and path-divergent: we see EUR/CHF more likely to grind toward 0.93-0.94 than to reach 0.95 cleanly, and we attach higher probability to retraced haven-squeeze interruptions than the consensus implies.
consensus_divergence.why_we_differ: The market and UBS underweight two structural offsets to the carry/de-escalation thesis: the persistent balance-of-payments surplus that mechanically bids CHF and keeps it chronically rich, and the fiscal-fortress comparative advantage that guarantees CHF attracts flows in any future DM stress. We also weight the crowded -37k short and CHF's violent-squeeze history more heavily as a near-term reversal risk than a clean trend-following consensus does.
consensus_divergence.payoff_if_right: If we are right, fading aggressive EUR/CHF-to-0.95 targets and hedging the squeeze tail avoids being stopped out on the sharp haven rallies that punctuate the grind, capturing the modest carry while preserving capital through the spikes.
consensus_divergence.risk_if_wrong: If consensus is right and EUR/CHF glides cleanly to 0.95 with no meaningful risk-off interruption, our hedged, low-conviction sizing leaves carry and directional upside on the table versus a naked long EUR/CHF position.

cleanest_bullish_expression.pair: USD/CHF (long CHF i.e. short USD/CHF) as a risk-off hedge
cleanest_bullish_expression.rationale: If the bullish-CHF case fires (risk-off, deal collapse), the cleanest expression of CHF strength is short USD/CHF, which captures both the haven bid and a potential USD softening. It is the most liquid CHF pair and the primary reference cross.

cleanest_bearish_expression.rationale: Long EUR/CHF is the cleanest bearish-CHF expression: it isolates the SNB-ECB rate gap and the ECB-hold tailwind, strips out USD noise, aligns with the SNB's own preference for a softer franc, and is the consensus carry trade. It is the pair the SNB cares most about, anchoring the trade to policy.

relative_value_pairs.thesis: Both are zero-to-low-yield havens; CHF/JPY isolates relative safe-haven and relative-carry demand. With JPY now out-yielding CHF and the BoJ slowly normalising, CHF/JPY can drift lower (JPY outperforming) - a relative-value short-CHF-vs-JPY trade that avoids a directional risk-regime bet.

The premier carry/risk barometer: long AUD/CHF expresses the risk-on, carry-friendly regime with the widest G10 rate gap (+435bps). It outperforms in the base case but is the first to reverse violently in risk-off, so it is the high-beta version of the bearish-CHF view.

cross_horizon_consistency.summary: Both horizons are modestly Bearish CHF at conviction 2, but the composition differs: tactical bearishness is driven by active risk-on and carry, strategic bearishness is a near-coin-flip where carry barely outweighs the structural surplus.
cross_horizon_consistency.divergence_explanation: The net scores differ sharply (tactical -0.286 vs strategic +0.013) even though both stances are Bearish: at tactical weights the risk/inflation factors pile onto the carry drag, while at strategic weights the external-balance surplus (re-weighted to 0.16) and fiscal fortress nearly neutralise the carry, leaving the strategic call a deliberate low-conviction override toward consensus rather than a score-driven verdict.
cross_horizon_consistency.inflection_point: The inflection is the durability of the risk-on regime: tactically de-escalation actively pressures CHF, but as the horizon lengthens the structural BoP and fiscal supports reassert and the recurrence of risk-off windows grows, flattening the score toward neutral.
cross_horizon_consistency.catalyst_for_transition: A risk-off shock (Iran deal collapse, DM stress) would flip both horizons CHF-bullish; persistent goldilocks plus sub-zero Swiss CPI would deepen both toward stronger bearish conviction.
cross_horizon_consistency.trading_implication: Run the same direction (small long EUR/CHF) across horizons but size to the low conviction and always pair with a cheap long-CHF gamma hedge - the strategic near-flat score warns that this is a carry-harvest with tail risk, not a high-conviction trend trade.

1m.annualised_pct: -2.3% (EUR/CHF lower in forwards = CHF richer; negative carry on a long-EUR/CHF position)

edge.1m: +58 pips (forward implies 0.9192, we expect 0.9250) - CHF weaker than forwards imply

forward_vs_spot.interpretation: The negative SNB-ECB carry makes CHF appreciate in the forwards (EUR/CHF forward points are negative, ~-2.3% annualised), so the forward curve embeds a CHF-stronger path. Our modestly Bearish-CHF view (EUR/CHF drifting toward 0.93-0.94) is therefore a bet against the forwards: we expect the franc to be weaker than the carry-implied forward curve. The edge widens with horizon (+58/+163/+295 pips at 1M/3M/6M), but it is precisely the carry you must overcome - a long EUR/CHF position pays away ~2.3% annualised, so the spot drift must beat that to profit, reinforcing the low-conviction, hedged sizing.

key_catalysts.event: SNB Monetary Policy Assessment (decision + conditional inflation forecast)
key_catalysts.rationale: All 35 economists expect a hold at 0.00%. The market focus is the tone and updated inflation forecast: a downward revision toward zero would revive negative-rate odds (CHF-bearish), while renewed intervention-readiness language would also cap CHF. A near-fully-priced event.

key_catalysts.rationale: Continued implementation of the Iran deal (signed ~14-15 Jun in Switzerland) and Hormuz reopening sustains risk-on and oil weakness, compressing CHF's haven premium. Any implementation hitch would partially re-arm the haven bid.

With May at +0.6% headline / +0.3% core, a print near zero or negative would revive SNB negative-rate speculation (CHF-bearish), while an upside surprise would ease that risk. The single most important domestic data point before the September SNB meeting.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Not specifically priced; market expects continued low single-digit-tenths inflation

key_catalysts.rationale: An ECB hold at 2.25% maintains the +225bps EUR-CHF rate gap that drives EUR/CHF higher (CHF-weaker) - 'music to the SNB's ears' per UBS. A surprise dovish ECB tilt would narrow the gap and support CHF.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Hold fully priced; tone is the variable

key_catalysts.rationale: A hawkish Fed hold keeps USD firm and the deep CH-US rate gap intact, supporting a higher USD/CHF (CHF-weaker). A dovish surprise would soften USD and support CHF on the USD/CHF cross.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Hold priced; hawkish bias partly priced after 17 Jun

key_catalysts.rationale: A second consecutive ultra-low or sub-zero print would intensify negative-rate speculation ahead of the September SNB meeting (CHF-bearish); a rebound would ease it. Confirms or breaks the disinflation trajectory.

Iranian state media flagged the toll-free Hormuz arrangement as lasting only ~60 days. The expiry/renegotiation is a binary re-escalation risk: a dispute could spike oil and reignite the CHF haven bid (CHF-bullish); a smooth extension sustains risk-on (CHF-bearish).

key_catalysts.rationale: Confirms whether sub-trend growth (SECO 2026 forecast 1.0%) is holding. A clear undershoot would add to the dovish-SNB case (CHF-bearish at the margin); resilient growth is neutral-to-mild-CHF-supportive.

The next quarterly decision. If disinflation has deepened toward zero, the SNB may face pressure to cut to negative or signal intervention (both CHF-bearish); a steady hold with in-band forecast is neutral. The pivotal strategic policy event.

key_catalysts.rationale: Final inflation read before the September SNB meeting plus the KOF leading indicator (98.0 in May). Together they set the SNB's reaction-function inputs; persistently weak readings tilt the SNB dovish (CHF-bearish).

key_catalysts.event: IMF/World Bank meetings & global risk-sentiment checkpoint
key_catalysts.rationale: A focal point for global growth/risk reassessment. A risk-off turn (DM fiscal worries, geopolitical relapse) would reignite the CHF haven bid (CHF-bullish); continued goldilocks sustains the bearish carry regime.

key_catalysts.event: SNB Monetary Policy Assessment (December, full Monetary Policy Report)
key_catalysts.rationale: The year-end decision with the comprehensive Monetary Policy Report. Sets the 2027 policy and inflation trajectory; the key strategic-horizon policy event and the most likely venue for any shift toward negative rates or intervention if disinflation persists.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Hold base case priced; policy-shift tail not priced

key_catalysts.event: Year-end USD funding / portfolio rebalancing window
key_catalysts.rationale: Seasonal year-end USD funding pressure can widen cross-currency basis and force CHF-funded carry unwinds (CHF-bullish); orderly conditions sustain the carry regime (CHF-bearish). A recurring strategic risk window.

invalidators.condition: SNB resumes large-scale FX intervention and CHF still fails to weaken
invalidators.mechanism: If sight deposits surge (intervention) yet CHF holds or strengthens, it signals overwhelming structural inflows that intervention cannot offset - a powerful CHF-bullish tell.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: SNB weekly sight deposits, FX reserves
invalidators.quantitative_threshold: Sight deposits rise > CHF 20bn/month with EUR/CHF not rising

invalidators.condition: ECB unexpectedly cuts, collapsing the SNB-ECB rate gap below 150bps
invalidators.mechanism: A narrower EUR-CHF rate gap removes the core carry rationale for a higher EUR/CHF, neutralising the dominant bearish-CHF driver.

invalidators.condition: Swiss CPI rebounds durably above 1.0% YoY, removing negative-rate risk and inviting SNB normalisation talk
invalidators.mechanism: A move toward the upper band would shift the SNB from dovish-tolerance to a neutral/hawkish bias, removing the CHF-bearish inflation undershoot and supporting the franc on rate-expectations.
invalidators.quantitative_threshold: Headline CPI > 1.0% YoY for two consecutive months

invalidators.condition: USD funding squeeze: 3M cross-currency basis widens sharply (EUR/USD basis below -40bps)
invalidators.mechanism: A dollar shortage forces global CHF-funded carry trades to unwind and triggers a haven bid, mechanically strengthening the franc - the classic left-tail CHF outcome.
invalidators.quantitative_threshold: 3M EUR/USD basis < -40bps or visible swap-line usage

invalidators.condition: Decisive risk-on entrenchment with EUR/CHF above 0.95 and the short base intact
invalidators.mechanism: A clean break above the consensus target with positioning not yet washed out would confirm and strengthen (not invalidate) the bearish view, warranting higher conviction rather than a reversal.
invalidators.quantitative_threshold: EUR/CHF > 0.95 weekly close with net specs not below -50k

factors_changed.reason: Unchanged direction - CHF remains the lowest-yield funder; strategic confidence trimmed as the SNB floor caps how much further the gap can widen and forwards embed CHF appreciation.

Material shift: re-grounded to June reality, ultra-low CPI (+0.6%/+0.3%) is now read as CHF-negative (removes SNB support, revives negative-rate risk) rather than a CHF-supportive disinflation credibility story.
The June US-Iran de-escalation and VIX collapse flipped the near-term risk read CHF-bearish (haven unwind), versus the more risk-neutral/CHF-supportive May backdrop.
Tactical softened to Neutral: with the June hold fully telegraphed and the SNB credibly in-band, guidance gives no directional steer rather than a bearish tilt.
The signed Iran peace deal (hosted in Switzerland) is now a concrete CHF-bearish de-escalation event near term, replacing the prior more balanced/haven-supportive geopolitical read.

signposts_triggered.implication: Consistent with the modest bearish-CHF lean and the consensus EUR/CHF-higher path.

conviction_change.explanation: The net stance flipped from Bullish CHF (May: tac +0.10, str +0.24) to Bearish CHF on both horizons, driven by the June risk-on de-escalation, the re-grounding of ultra-low inflation as CHF-negative, and alignment with the consensus EUR/CHF-higher path. Strategic conviction eased from 3 to 2 because the near-flat strategic score (+0.013) reflects the structural surplus/fiscal supports nearly offsetting the carry drag - a genuine coin-flip rather than a confident view.

new_catalysts: US-Iran peace framework signed in Switzerland (~14-15 Jun) and Hormuz reopening - the dominant new CHF-bearish de-escalation event
Hormuz 60-day toll-free window expiry (~mid-August) - a new binary re-escalation risk window
UBS capital-rules ordinances due January 2027 - a new banking-stability item (~$20bn extra requirement)

seasonality.pattern: CHF has no strong calendar seasonality comparable to JPY fiscal-year-end repatriation; the relevant recurring pattern is year-end USD funding/portfolio-rebalancing pressure that can widen cross-currency basis and force CHF-funded carry unwinds (CHF-supportive).
seasonality.relevance_window: December 2026 year-end - at the far edge of the strategic horizon
seasonality.historical_magnitude: Modest and inconsistent; year-end funding squeezes occasionally produce sharp but brief CHF strength via carry unwind
seasonality.current_alignment: Not currently active in June; flagged as a strategic-horizon risk window rather than a present driver

assumptions: The SNB held its policy rate at 0.00% on 18 June 2026 as universally expected (Reuters poll: all 35 economists); analysis is written on that base case. If the SNB surprised (cut to negative or signalled intervention), the rate/guidance factors would shift more CHF-bearish.

Oil and risk-sentiment levels use live post-deal references (Brent <$80, VIX ~16.2) rather than the data cache's pre-deal oil print (~$95 WTI / ~$97 Brent dated 2026-06-08), which is superseded by the ~14-15 June US-Iran framework.

Swiss sovereign yields (2Y ~-0.05%, 10Y ~0.35%) are estimated from the 0% SNB rate and Switzerland's structurally lowest-in-G10 curve; precise live Confederation yields were not in the cache and are flagged as best-available estimates - Factor 1 confidence is tempered accordingly.

Current account is reported at the OECD cache value of +3.0% of GDP (Q4-2025); the underlying Swiss surplus on national definitions adjusting for gold-trade distortions is materially larger (historically 5-8%), and the external-balance factor reflects that structural reading.

Implied vol, risk-reversal and cross-currency basis levels for CHF are estimated from the low-vol G10 regime and the SNB event; precise live option/basis surfaces were not in the free-stack cache and are flagged as best-available, with Factor 9/10 confidence set conservatively.
EUR/CHF, GBP/CHF, CHF/JPY, AUD/CHF and CAD/CHF crosses are computed from the cache's USD-pair closes (2026-06-17); forward points are estimated from the SNB-ECB carry and flagged as indicative.
